subject: Physics
chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Three photons originating from an excited atomic-hydrogen sample are detected. Their energies are $12.1 \text{ eV}$, $10.2 \text{ eV}$, and $1.9 \text{ eV}$. These photons must have been emitted by
options: either two atoms or three atoms
questions.solution:
The energy levels of a hydrogen atom are given by $E_n = -\dfrac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$.

The energies of the first few levels are:
$E_1 = -13.6 \text{ eV}$
$E_2 = -3.4 \text{ eV}$
$E_3 = -1.51 \text{ eV}$

The energy differences between these levels are:
$E_3 - E_1 = -1.51 - (-13.6) = 12.09 \text{ eV} \approx 12.1 \text{ eV}$
$E_2 - E_1 = -3.4 - (-13.6) = 10.2 \text{ eV}$
$E_3 - E_2 = -1.51 - (-3.4) = 1.89 \text{ eV} \approx 1.9 \text{ eV}$

The detected photons correspond to the transitions $n=3 \to n=1$ ($12.1 \text{ eV}$), $n=2 \to n=1$ ($10.2 \text{ eV}$), and $n=3 \to n=2$ ($1.9 \text{ eV}$).

A single hydrogen atom in the $n=3$ state can either transition directly to $n=1$ (emitting one photon of $12.1 \text{ eV}$) or transition to $n=2$ and then to $n=1$ (emitting two photons of $1.9 \text{ eV}$ and $10.2 \text{ eV}$). It cannot emit all three photons as it only has one electron that eventually reaches the ground state.

To observe all three photons, we need at least two atoms:
Case 1 (Two atoms): One atom undergoes the $3 \to 1$ transition, and the second atom undergoes the $3 \to 2 \to 1$ transitions.
Case 2 (Three atoms): One atom undergoes $3 \to 1$, another undergoes $3 \to 2$, and a third undergoes $2 \to 1$.

Therefore, the photons must have been emitted by either two atoms or three atoms.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Among the following transitions, which one results in the minimum wavelength?
questions.solution:
The energy of a photon emitted during a transition from $n_2$ to $n_1$ is given by Rydberg's formula:

$\Delta E = 13.6 Z^2 \left( \dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2} \right)$ eV

The wavelength of the emitted photon is inversely proportional to the energy difference, since $\Delta E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$. Thus, the minimum wavelength corresponds to the maximum energy difference.

Calculating the proportional energy difference for each given transition:

For $n = 5$ to $n = 4$: $\Delta E \propto \dfrac{1}{4^2} - \dfrac{1}{5^2} = \dfrac{9}{400} = 0.0225$

For $n = 4$ to $n = 3$: $\Delta E \propto \dfrac{1}{3^2} - \dfrac{1}{4^2} = \dfrac{7}{144} \approx 0.0486$

For $n = 3$ to $n = 2$: $\Delta E \propto \dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{3^2} = \dfrac{5}{36} \approx 0.1389$

For $n = 2$ to $n = 1$: $\Delta E \propto \dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{2^2} = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75$

The transition from $n = 2$ to $n = 1$ has the maximum energy difference and therefore results in the minimum wavelength.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Among the following systems, which will have the minimum radius for the first orbit ($n = 1$)?
questions.solution:
The radius of the $n$-th Bohr orbit for a hydrogen-like species is given by:

$r_n = \dfrac{0.529 \times n^2}{Z}$ \AA

For the first orbit, $n = 1$. Thus, the radius is inversely proportional to the atomic number $Z$ ($r_1 \propto \dfrac{1}{Z}$).

To have the minimum radius, the system must have the maximum atomic number $Z$.

For doubly ionized lithium ($Li^{2+}$), $Z = 3$
For deuterium atom ($D$), $Z = 1$
For singly ionized helium ($He^+$), $Z = 2$
For hydrogen atom ($H$), $Z = 1$

Since $Li^{2+}$ has the highest atomic number ($Z = 3$), it will have the minimum radius for the first orbit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: For an electron in a hydrogen atom, the minimum orbital angular momentum is given by
questions.solution:
According to Bohr's atomic model, the orbital angular momentum of an electron in a hydrogen atom is quantized and is given by

$L = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}$

where $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$

For the minimum orbital angular momentum, the principal quantum number $n$ must be minimum, i.e., $n = 1$.

$L_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{h}{2\pi}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Assume an electron in a hydrogen atom undergoes a transition from $n = 3$ to $n = 2$ in $10^{-8} \text{ s}$. Using the standard relation between torque and angular momentum, the order of magnitude of the torque acting on the electron during this period is
questions.solution:
The angular momentum of an electron in the $n$-th orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by $L = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}$.

The change in angular momentum during the transition from $n = 3$ to $n = 2$ is

$\Delta L = L_3 - L_2 = \dfrac{3h}{2\pi} - \dfrac{2h}{2\pi} = \dfrac{h}{2\pi}$

Substituting the value of Planck constant $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$, we get

$\Delta L = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \pi} \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$

The average torque acting on the electron is the rate of change of angular momentum:

$\tau = \dfrac{\Delta L}{\Delta t}$

Given the transition time $\Delta t = 10^{-8} \text{ s}$, the torque is

$\tau = \dfrac{1.05 \times 10^{-34}}{10^{-8}} = 1.05 \times 10^{-26} \text{ N m}$

The order of magnitude of the torque is $10^{-26} \text{ N m}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: For which of the following systems is the wavelength associated with the transition from $n = 2$ to $n = 1$ the minimum?
questions.solution:
The wavelength of a spectral line for a hydrogen-like species is given by the Rydberg formula:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R_{H} Z^{2} \left( \dfrac{1}{n_{1}^{2}} - \dfrac{1}{n_{2}^{2}} \right)$

For the transition from $n = 2$ to $n = 1$, $n_{1} = 1$ and $n_{2} = 2$. Thus, the formula becomes:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R_{H} Z^{2} \left( \dfrac{1}{1^{2}} - \dfrac{1}{2^{2}} \right) = \dfrac{3}{4} R_{H} Z^{2}$

From this relation, it is clear that $\lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{Z^{2}}$.

To obtain the minimum wavelength, the atomic number $Z$ must be maximum.

For doubly ionized lithium ($Li^{2+}$), $Z = 3$.
For singly ionized helium ($He^{+}$), $Z = 2$.
For deuterium atom ($D$), $Z = 1$.
For hydrogen atom ($H$), $Z = 1$.

Since doubly ionized lithium has the maximum value of $Z$, the wavelength associated with the transition from $n = 2$ to $n = 1$ is minimum for it.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Identify the curve from the figure below that could depict the speed of an electron in a hydrogen atom as a function of its principal quantum number $n$.
questions.solution:
The speed of an electron in the $n^{\text{th}}$ Bohr orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by:

$v_n = \dfrac{Z e^2}{2 \epsilon_0 n h}$

For a hydrogen atom, $Z = 1$, which gives:

$v_n \propto \dfrac{1}{n}$

This indicates that the speed $v$ is inversely proportional to the principal quantum number $n$. The graph of an inversely proportional relationship ($y \propto \dfrac{1}{x}$) is a rectangular hyperbola.

Analyzing the given curves:
Curve a shows an increasing trend.
Curve b shows a linear decrease.
Curve c represents a rectangular hyperbola, showing a rapid decrease that gradually flattens out, which matches $v \propto \dfrac{1}{n}$.
Curve d shows an increasing trend.

Thus, curve c correctly depicts the variation of $v$ with $n$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: In a one-electron system, the radius of the shortest orbit is given as $18 \text{ pm}$. This system could be
questions.solution:
The radius of the $n$-th orbit for a hydrogen-like species is given by:

$r_n = \dfrac{52.9 n^2}{Z} \text{ pm}$

For the shortest orbit, $n = 1$. Given that the radius is $18 \text{ pm}$, we can find the atomic number $Z$:

$18 = \dfrac{52.9 (1)^2}{Z}$

$Z = \dfrac{52.9}{18} \approx 2.94$

The closest integer value is $Z = 3$, which corresponds to Lithium. Since it is a one-electron system, the species must be $\text{Li}^{++}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: When a hydrogen atom in the ground state absorbs $10.2 \text{ eV}$ of energy, the orbital angular momentum of the electron increases by
questions.solution:
The energy of an electron in the $n^{\text{th}}$ orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by $E_n = -\dfrac{13.6}{n^2} \text{ eV}$.

For the ground state ($n=1$), $E_1 = -13.6 \text{ eV}$.

When the electron absorbs $10.2 \text{ eV}$ of energy, its new energy becomes:

$E_n = -13.6 + 10.2 = -3.4 \text{ eV}$

Equating this to the energy expression for the $n^{\text{th}}$ orbit:

$-\dfrac{13.6}{n^2} = -3.4$

$n^2 = \dfrac{13.6}{3.4} = 4 \Rightarrow n = 2$

According to Bohr's model, the orbital angular momentum of an electron in the $n^{\text{th}}$ orbit is $L = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}$.

The increase in orbital angular momentum is:

$\Delta L = L_2 - L_1 = \dfrac{2h}{2\pi} - \dfrac{h}{2\pi} = \dfrac{h}{2\pi}$

Substituting the values of $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$ and $\pi = 3.14$:

$\Delta L = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 3.14} \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: For all hydrogen-like atoms and ions in their ground states, which of the following parameters is identical?
options: Orbital angular momentum of the electron
questions.solution:
For hydrogen-like atoms and ions in the ground state, the principal quantum number $n = 1$.

The radius of the orbit is given by $r = 0.529 \dfrac{n^2}{Z}$ \AA, which depends on the atomic number $Z$.

The energy of the atom is given by $E = -13.6 \dfrac{Z^2}{n^2}$ eV, which depends on $Z$.

The speed of the electron is given by $v = 2.18 \times 10^6 \dfrac{Z}{n}$ m/s, which depends on $Z$.

The orbital angular momentum of the electron in the ground state ($1s$ orbital, $l = 0$) is $\sqrt{l(l+1)} \dfrac{h}{2\pi} = 0$. According to Bohr's theory, the angular momentum is $\dfrac{nh}{2\pi} = \dfrac{h}{2\pi}$. In both models, it is independent of $Z$ and depends only on the quantum numbers $n$ or $l$.

Thus, the orbital angular momentum is identical for all hydrogen-like atoms and ions in their ground states.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: As higher values of $n$ are considered for orbits in a hydrogen atom, the electric potential energy of the atom
questions.solution:
The electric potential energy of an electron in the $n$-th orbit of a hydrogen atom is given by $U = -\dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{e^2}{r_n}$.

The radius of the $n$-th orbit is directly proportional to the square of the principal quantum number, $r_n \propto n^2$.

Substituting this relation, we get $U \propto -\dfrac{1}{n^2}$.

As $n$ increases, the magnitude $\dfrac{1}{n^2}$ decreases. Due to the negative sign, the overall value of the potential energy becomes less negative, which means it increases.

Therefore, as higher values of $n$ are considered, the electric potential energy of the atom increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: Within a laser tube, every photon
questions.solution:
Within a laser tube, both spontaneous and stimulated emissions occur.

Spontaneous emission produces photons in random directions with a slight spread in energy and wavelength due to natural and Doppler broadening mechanisms. Stimulated emission produces photons that travel in the same direction with the same energy and wavelength as the incident photons.

Because spontaneous emission photons are also present within the tube, not every photon travels in the same direction, nor do they all possess the exact same energy or wavelength.

However, all photons are quanta of electromagnetic radiation and travel at the same speed (the speed of light) in the given medium.

Therefore, within a laser tube, every photon travels at the same speed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Bohr's Model and Physics of the Atom
questions.q: An atom (or ion) has a ground state energy of $-54.4 \text{ eV}$. This species could be
questions.solution:
The energy of an electron in the $n^{\text{th}}$ orbit of a hydrogen-like species is given by $E_n = -13.6 \dfrac{Z^2}{n^2} \text{ eV}$.

For the ground state, $n = 1$.

$E_1 = -13.6 Z^2 \text{ eV}$

Given $E_1 = -54.4 \text{ eV}$.

$-13.6 Z^2 = -54.4$

$Z^2 = \dfrac{54.4}{13.6} = 4$

$Z = 2$

The species with atomic number $Z = 2$ is helium. Since the Bohr model applies to single-electron species, the ion is $\text{He}^+$.
